name: Medb Extended
description: Medb, also spelled Meadhbh, is a powerful and complex figure in Irish mythology, primarily known from the Ulster Cycle. Queen of Connacht, she is depicted as a fierce warrior queen, driven by ambition, lust for power, and a relentless desire to maintain her status. Medb is not merely a ruler; she is a personification of sovereignty, choosing her kings and wielding considerable influence over the land. Her most famous exploit is undoubtedly the Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley), where she leads the armies of Connacht against Ulster to capture the Brown Bull of Cooley, driven by a desire to equal her husband Ailill's wealth. Medb's character is multifaceted; she is cunning, strategic, and possesses a strong will, but also displays ruthlessness and a willingness to use any means necessary to achieve her goals. Her relationships are often fraught with conflict, particularly those with men, whom she frequently dominates. She embodies a powerful, independent female figure in a patriarchal society, challenging traditional gender roles and asserting her dominance through both political maneuvering and military prowess. Her reign is marked by constant warfare and ambition, reflecting the turbulent and heroic age of Irish mythology. Despite her often-negative portrayal, Medb remains a compelling and significant figure, representing female power, ambition, and the complexities of leadership in a world of warriors and kings.
subtitle: Fierce Queen of Connacht

keyMyths.title: The Cattle Raid of Cooley (Táin Bó Cúailnge)
keyMyths.description: Medb's most famous exploit, she leads the armies of Connacht against Ulster to steal the Brown Bull of Cooley, driven by a desire to equal her husband's wealth. The raid results in a long and bloody conflict, showcasing Medb's ambition and strategic mind.

keyMyths.title: Medb's Choice of Kings
keyMyths.description: Medb is known for her ability to choose her own kings, asserting her dominance and control over the kingship of Connacht. This highlights her power and influence in a patriarchal society.

keyMyths.title: The Death of Medb
keyMyths.description: Medb's death is a somewhat ignominious one; she is killed by Furbaide Ferbend, Conchobar's son, with a piece of cheese while bathing in a pool. This emphasizes the unpredictable nature of fate in Irish mythology.

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation and retelling of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.
sources.text: A modern translation and retelling of the Táin Bó Cúailnge.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: A collection of translated Irish myths and sagas, including stories of Medb.
sources.text: A collection of translated Irish myths and sagas, including stories of Medb.
